Title: Crystal structure of fatty acid thioesterase A bound by 129 fragments provides diverse development opportunities

Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: fatty acid thioesterase A; acyl-acyl carrier protein thioesterase
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: plastid de novo fatty acid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: fatty acyl-ACP chain termination / oleoyl-ACP hydrolysis step
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control point for amount and length of fatty acids present; not explicitly established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: oleoyl-ACP (physiological/modelled); oleoyl-CoA (surrogate substrate in vitro assay)
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: free fatty acid (notably C18:1/oleic acid) plus ACP; CoA released from oleoyl-CoA surrogate in assay
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: hydrolyzes long-chain acyl thioesters, terminating de novo fatty acid synthesis by releasing fatty acids from ACP cofactors; study models C18:1 substrate processing
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: oleic acid / free C18:1 fatty acid
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: major lipid building block for downstream lipid metabolism including acyl-CoA formation, triacylglycerols and very-long-chain fatty acids
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: plastid-produced fatty acids subsequently feed cellular lipid pools; specific accumulation organ not reported
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: plant acyl-ACP thioesterase; tandem Hotdog-fold thioesterase
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: FatA terminates plastid fatty acid biosynthesis by releasing fatty acids, with substrate-binding and catalytic features that can be inhibited by herbicide-like ligands and fragments
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. The study solved the crystal structure of Arabidopsis thaliana FatA and showed it forms a physiological dimer with tandem Hotdog folds. 2. Structural modelling and fragment screening support FatA as the enzyme that hydrolyzes C18:1 acyl substrate at the terminal step of plastid fatty acid biosynthesis. 3. A crystallographic fragment screen identified 129 fragment-bound structures and 141 binding events across herbicide, active, and dimer-interface sites. 4. Ten fragments showed measurable inhibition in vitro, and optimization of fragment x1816 produced x1816-FU1 with ~90 nM binding affinity. 5. The N-terminal substrate-binding region is highly conformationally dynamic, revealing inhibitor-development opportunities.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: X-ray crystallography; crystallographic fragment screening; structural modelling of FatA-ACP-substrate complex; in vitro enzyme inhibition assay; surface plasmon resonance
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: fragment soaking in DMSO for crystallographic screening; in vitro inhibitor treatment
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Inhibition of FatA alone may be insufficient for herbicidal lethality because the paper states both FatA and FatB likely need to be inhibited; possible differential inhibition versus FatB due to N-terminal domain variation
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Primarily a chemical biology/herbicide target rather than breeding target; structural information enables rational design of new herbicides targeting plastid fatty acid termination
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Catalytic residues discussed include Asp262, Asn264, His266, Asn269 and Glu300. Herbicide/substrate binding site centers on Arg176. PDB IDs include 9HRQ and 9HRR; herbicide complexes 9GRR, 9GS1, 9HMT.
